errors: 1) The value set for “Reason for not performing Chlamydia Screening” has an ID of “TBD”, which is a placeholder and not a valid, resolvable value set identifier. 2) The “In Demographic” expression restricts eligible patients to females only, whereas current screening guidelines recommend screening all sexually active individuals aged 16‑24, regardless of sex.

[The age threshold is set to 40 years, whereas current guidelines (USPSTF, ACS) recommend initiating routine mammography at 50 years (or 45 for ACS) for average‑risk women. The screening interval is set to 1 year; most guidelines advise a 2‑year interval for average‑risk women, with 1‑year intervals reserved for higher‑risk populations.]

The logic uses a 1‑month lookback period for colonoscopy procedures to determine whether a screening is needed, which is not clinically reasonable (the recommended interval for colonoscopy screening is 10 years). Consequently, the “Colonoscopy” expression incorrectly flags patients who have had a colonoscopy within the last month as needing a new screening, and the inclusion criteria (age ≥ 50 AND no colonoscopy in the last month) will incorrectly identify many patients as eligible for screening.
["Lookback interval of 1 month is too short for prostate cancer screening; guidelines recommend annual screening."]

The logic excludes women who have an osteopenia diagnosis from screening (the “Not Has Osteopenia Diagnosis” condition), yet USPSTF guidelines recommend that women aged 65 years or older be screened for osteoporosis regardless of osteopenia status.

[Threshold for HbA1c is set to 9 g% which is higher than the standard 6.5 % diagnostic threshold and uses an incorrect unit (g% instead of %). The MostRecent function sorts observations ascending, which would return the earliest rather than the most recent observation.]

; The logic for **Has Smoking History** only checks for an active Condition coded as “Tobacco User”; it does not enforce the guideline requirement of a ≥20 pack‑year smoking history or a quit status within the last 15 years.; The **In Demographic** age range (50–80 years) is correct, but the recommendation’s rationale references a 20 pack‑year history that is not reflected in the ELM expressions.; The **Has Recent LDCT** check uses the Observation’s `issued` date to determine a recent scan, which is acceptable, but the overall screening trigger does not consider whether the patient has quit smoking within the last 15 years, a key USPSTF criterion.; Consequently, the CDS may incorrectly recommend screening to patients who do not meet the full USPSTF criteria (e.g., those with a smoking history <20 pack‑years or who quit >15 years ago).

The guideline for average‑risk colorectal cancer screening recommends starting at age 45 (or 50 in older guidelines) and continuing through age 75, not 60–75 as defined in the ELM. Additionally, the ELM uses a single 2‑year interval for all screening modalities, whereas current recommendations specify different intervals (e.g., colonoscopy every 10 years, FIT annually, stool DNA every 3 years, etc.).

The BMI threshold for obesity is set at ≥35 kg/m², whereas most clinical guidelines for prediabetes screening target patients with BMI ≥ 30 kg/m² (obesity). This value is clinically too high and would exclude many patients who should be screened.
Age range mismatch – the ELM defines the target population as women aged 30–65 years, whereas the CPG (USPSTF) recommends cervical cancer screening for women aged 21–65 years.

The age threshold used in the “In Demographic” expression is 75 years, whereas USPSTF and most falls‑prevention guidelines recommend screening adults **65 years or older**. This mismatch makes the clinical logic not fully aligned with the guideline.
["In Demographic age threshold is 25, but USPSTF recommends screening adults 18 years or older."]
The age range used in the ELM (55–75 years) does not match the USPSTF guideline, which recommends AAA screening for men aged 65–75 years who have ever smoked.

The look‑back interval for mammography is set to 1 year, whereas most breast‑cancer screening guidelines (e.g., USPSTF, ACS) recommend a 2‑year interval for women aged 40–74. This value is therefore not clinically reasonable.

["Missing upper age limit (should be 80 years)", "No check for 20 pack-year smoking history", "No consideration of quit status within last 15 years", "Logic for 'InPopulation' does not align with the rationale that requires 20 pack-year history and age 50-80"]

The logic does not restrict to male patients; the “Is Male” expression is defined but not used in the “Needs AAA Screening” expression. This makes the recommendation apply to all genders, which is not clinically appropriate per USPSTF guidelines.

[The ProcedureLookBack function uses a LookBack interval of 1 month, which is far too short for colon cancer screening (the guideline requires a 10‑year interval). Consequently, the inclusion criteria “Colonoscopy” incorrectly requires a colonoscopy within the last month rather than the absence of a colonoscopy in the last 10 years, making the logic clinically unreasonable.]

The “Needs Falls Prevention Screening” expression requires that the patient has a recent falls assessment, yet the recommendation is to perform a falls risk assessment. This logic is clinically unreasonable because a recent assessment should preclude the need for another assessment. Additionally, the indicator “warning” may not align with standard CDS indicator levels (e.g., “alert” or “info” are more typical).

[The logic for Needs Depression Screening incorrectly excludes patients with either bipolar or depression diagnosis. It uses NOT (Has Bipolar AND Has Depression) instead of NOT (Has Bipolar OR Has Depression), which may incorrectly recommend screening for patients with a single diagnosis. Also it excludes patients with both diagnoses, which is not clinically indicated.]
